content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Alfredo     Del     Dan
      Barbara    Ken G.  Mickey
      Rich M.    Ed      Jeanne
      Fred            Mike
                 Gina    Ted
      Bob S.     John K.
                 Rich E.  Dawn
                 Jay

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ken Finney

Subject:

     It was determined at the Opinion Request meeting of
January 14, 1977, that a trustee who only has legal title
to the property constituting the corpus of a trust and
possesses no beneficial interest in the trust need not
disclose the investments of the trust on his Statement
of Economic Interests. It also was decided that this in-
terpretation should be formalized in a regulation but
that such a regulation should await a general considera-
tion of the problems posed by trusts.

KF:jo

Date : Jan. 17, 1977

7720J-139

2.6

FROM: Don H. Vickers, Deputy County Counsel

RE: Conflict of Interest - Disclosure Statement

You have asked whether you must disclose interests in a trust in which your wife has a future interest. She will become the sole beneficiary of the trust in the event she survives her father who is currently the beneficiary of the trust. The trust is administered in the State of Washington. It owns no real property in this county. It does own stock in various corporations some of which probably do business in this county.

Based upon the foregoing it appears to us that you need not disclose your wife's interest in the trust; however, particular investments of the trust must be disclosed if certain conditions are found to exist. Those conditions are:

1. Your wife's interest in the trust is at least 10%;

2. Her pro rata share of a particular investment is at least $1,000;

3. The particular business entity in which stock is owned has an interest in real property within the jurisdiction or does, or plans to do, business in the county;

4. The particular business entity comes within one of the disclosure categories which is applicable to your position.

Section 92034 of the Government Code and Definition #12 of Appendix C of the Health Department's Conflict of Interest Code defines an

Dr. Lawrence Hart
Page Two
May 12, 1977

investment as including any financial interest in a business enti...
"if the business entity or any parent, subsidiary or otherwise re-
lated business entity has an interest in real property within the
jurisdiction of the business or plans to do business within the
jurisdiction of the agency at any time any statement or other acti...
is required under this Code." A "business entity" means any organi-
zation or enterprise operated for profit including a "business trus"
Because the trust does not own property or do business in the "juri...
diction" as defined in Definition 13 of Appendix C of the Code we
do not believe it has to be reported.

Section 82034 and Definition 12 also provide "investments of an
individual includes a pro rata share of investments of any business
entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater. W...
believe this means if the trust owns investments which would be po...
portable if owned by your wife directly your wife's pro rata intere...
must be reported if she has a 10% or greater interest in the trust
and the value of her interest in a particular investment of the
trust exceeds $1,000. In determining whether or not your wife has
a 10% interest in the trust you should determine as best you can
the current value of your wife's future interest.

The foregoing represents our best opinion. In the course of our
research on the subject we called the FPPC to determine if they had
rendered an opinion on whether a future interest in a trust could
give rise to disclosure of assets of the trust. We were advised
they had not rendered such an opinion but a staff member stated he
believed no such disclosure would be required until the beneficiary
interest becomes a present interest. We intend to request a formal
opinion of the commission; however, since such an opinion could not
be obtained prior to the deadline for the initial statement our
advice at the present time is as specified herein.

DAV:hh

sections.question: Whether you must disclose interests in a trust in which your wife has a future interest, where she will become the sole beneficiary of the trust in the event she survives her father who is currently the beneficiary of the trust, the trust is administered in the State of Washington, owns no real property in this state, but does own stock in various corporations some of which probably do business in this county.
sections.conclusion: You need not disclose your wife's interest in the trust; however, particular investments of the trust must be disclosed if certain conditions are found to exist: (1) Your wife's interest in the trust is at least 10%; (2) Her pro rata share of a particular investment is at least $1,000; (3) The particular business entity in which stock is owned has an interest in real property within the jurisdiction or does, or plans to do, business in the county; (4) The particular business entity comes within one of the disclosure categories which is applicable to your position.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a county counsel disclose his wife's future interest in an out-of-state trust that owns securities in corporations doing business in the county?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The wife's general interest in the trust need not be disclosed, but specific trust investments must be reported if the wife has a 10% or greater interest in the trust, her pro rata share of a particular investment exceeds $1,000, and the investment meets other statutory criteria related to business within the jurisdiction.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple letters/memos. Primary content is advice letter from Don H. Vickers, Deputy County Counsel to Dr. Lawrence Hart dated May 12, 1977, with FPPC memorandum (dated January 17, 1977) from Ken Finney regarding trustee disclosure obligations included as background. Question and conclusion sections synthesized from letter content as formal sections not explicitly marked.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether you must disclose interests in a trust in which your wife has a future interest, where she will become the sole beneficiary of the trust in the event she survives her father who is currently the beneficiary of the trust, the trust is administered in the State of Washington, owns no real property in this state, but does own stock in various corporations some of which probably do business in this county.

CONCLUSION: You need not disclose your wife's interest in the trust; however, particular investments of the trust must be disclosed if certain conditions are found to exist: (1) Your wife's interest in the trust is at least 10%; (2) Her pro rata share of a particular investment is at least $1,000; (3) The particular business entity in which stock is owned has an interest in real property within the jurisdiction or does, or plans to do, business in the county; (4) The particular business entity comes within one of the disclosure categories which is applicable to your position.
embedding.first_500_words: This is a memorandum from the Fair Political Practices Commission, dated January 17, 1977. The memo is addressed to several individuals, including Alfredo, Del, Dan, Barbara, Ken G., Mickey, Rich M., Ed, Jeanne, Fred, Mike, Gina, Ted, Bob S., John K., Rich E., Dawn, and Jay. The memo is signed by Ken Finney. The subject of the memo states: It was determined at the Opinion Request meeting of January 14, 1977, that a trustee who only has legal title to the property constituting the corpus of a trust and possesses no beneficial interest in the trust need not disclose the investments of the trust on his Statement of Economic Interests. It also was decided that this interpretation should be formalized in a regulation but that such a regulation should await a general consideration of the problems posed by trusts. KF:jo FROM: Don H. Vickers, Deputy County Counsel RE: Conflict of Interest - Disclosure Statement You have asked whether you must disclose interests in a trust in which your wife has a future interest. She will become the sole beneficiary of the trust in the event she survives her father who is currently the beneficiary of the trust. The trust is administered in the State of Washington. It owns no real property in this state. It does own stock in various corporations some of which probably do business in this county. Based upon the foregoing it appears to us that you need not disclose your wife's interest in the trust; however, particular investments of the trust must be disclosed if certain conditions are found to exist. Those conditions are: 1. Your wife's interest in the trust is at least 10%; 2. Her pro rata share of a particular investment is at least $1,000; 3. The particular business entity in which stock is owned has an interest in real property within the jurisdiction or does, or plans to do, business in the county; 4. The particular business entity comes within one of the disclosure categories which is applicable to your position. Section 82034 of the Government Code and Definition #12 of Appendix C of the Health Department's Conflict of Interest Code defines an Dr. Lawrence Hart Page Two May 12, 1977 investment as including any financial interest in a business entity "if the business entity or any parent, subsidiary or otherwise related business entity has an interest in real property within the jurisdiction, or does business or plans to do business within the jurisdiction of the agency at any time any statement or other action is required under this Code." A "business entity" means any organization or enterprise operated for profit including a "business trust." Because the trust does not own property or do business in the "jurisdiction" as defined in Definition 13 of Appendix C of the Code we do not believe it has to be reported. Section 82034 and Definition 12 also provide "investments of an individual includes a pro rata share of investments of any business entity or trust
embedding.summary: FPPC advises that a County Counsel need not disclose his wife's future interest in an out-of-state trust itself, but must disclose particular trust investments meeting four specified conditions regarding her interest percentage, investment value, business location, and disclosure category applicability.
